title: Arena of Valor - Wikipedia
content: The overall gameplay of *Arena of Valor* highly resembles *League of Legends*, a MOBA game on PC developed and published by Riot Games, which is also a subsidiary of Tencent and a sister company to TiMi. Players control characters known as heroes, each possessing a unique set of abilities. On November 21, 2016, Garena launched *Arena of Valor* in Vietnam and become one of the most successful mobile games in this country. Morefun Studios (which is also another of Tencent's video game development studio) developed and released a mobile MOBA named *War Song* on January 22, 2018, in an attempt to compete with *Arena of Valor*. 4. **^** "League of Legends and Arena of Valor are among eSports titles at 2018 Asian Games". **^** "Report: Tencent giving up on Arena of Valor in the West and is now working together with Riot Games to develop League of Legends for mobile".

title: What is AOV? Understanding Average Order Value ...
content: For CPG brands, AOV serves as an essential tool for understanding customer purchasing patterns and evaluating the effectiveness of pricing strategies. For example, if a CPG brand generates $300,000 in revenue from 6,000 orders, the AOV is $50. While AOV tracks the average value of a single transaction, Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) measures the total revenue a customer generates over the entire duration of their relationship with a brand. For example, a brand with an AOV of $60 and a CLV of $600 can use these insights to optimize both immediate sales and overall customer value. SimplyDepo plays a vital role in helping CPG brands monitor and improve their Average Order Value (AOV). Through its comprehensive analytics, SimplyDepo helps track how promotional efforts and product assortments impact transaction values, allowing brands to fine-tune their strategies to boost AOV. Average Order Value (AOV) is a crucial metric for CPG brands seeking to understand customer behavior and optimize sales strategies.

title: Glasgow Coma Scale - Wikipedia
content: The **Glasgow Coma Scale** (**GCS**) is a clinical diagnostic tool widely used since the 1970s to assess a patient's level of consciousness. While initially primarily utilized in patients with traumatic brain injuries, its utilization has extended to assess the level of consciousness in a wide range of settings, illnesses, and injuries.The GCS score takes into consideration three components: eye movements, verbal response (e.g., speech), and motor response (e.g. purposeful body movements). The Glasgow Coma Scale is reported as the combined score (which ranges from 3 to 15) and the score of each test (E for eye, V for Verbal, and M for Motor). "Performance of the Pediatric Glasgow Coma Scale Score in the Evaluation of Children With Blunt Head Trauma". "Inter-rater reliability of the Full Outline of UnResponsiveness score and the Glasgow Coma Scale in critically ill patients: a prospective observational study". "The conundrum of the Glasgow Coma Scale in intubated patients: a linear regression prediction of the Glasgow verbal score from the Glasgow eye and motor scores".

title: Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): What It Is, Interpretation & Chart
content: # Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS). The Glasgow Coma Scale is a tool that healthcare providers use to measure decreases in consciousness. The scores from each section of the scale are useful for describing disruptions in nervous system function and also help providers track changes. The Glasgow Coma Scale scores eye, movement and speech abilities to determine how conscious you are. The Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) is a system to “score” or measure how conscious you are. To get your Glasgow Coma Scale score, providers take the scores from the three categories of the GCS and add them together. The GCS-P score still uses a score of 8 or fewer to mean a coma. When providers use the GCS in connection with a head injury, they tend to apply scoring ranges to describe how severe the injury is. The Glasgow Coma Scale is the most common tool healthcare providers use to measure decreases in consciousness and comas.

title: Adult Glasgow Coma Scale - Medscape Reference
content: The Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) is used to describe the general level of consciousness in patients with traumatic brain injury (TBI) and to define broad categories of head injury. [1;)] The GCS is divided into 3 categories, eye opening (E), motor response (M), and verbal response (V). GCS score = E + M + V. Also see Medscape’s Glasgow Coma Scale Calculator. ### Best verbal response scores. In intubated patients, the maximum GCS score is 10T and the minimum score is 2T. Mild head injuries are generally defined as those associated with a GCS score of 13-15, and moderate head injuries are those associated with a GCS score of 9-12. A GCS score of 8 or less defines a severe head injury. Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy for Traumatic Brain Injury: Evidence-Based Medicine.
